coredata.dc:title: MoS2 QDs-Based sensor for measurement of fluazinam with triple signal output
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xianfeng
Hou, Jingzhou
Lan, Shiyu
Shen, Caihong
Huo, Danqun
Ji, Zhong
Ma, Yi
Luo, Huibo
Zhang, Suyi
He, Qiang
Hou, Changjun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, direct detection of fluazinam was realized using a fluorescent sensor using disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) via inner filter effect (IFE). The maximum excitation of as-prepared MoS2 QDs presented a complementary spectral-overlap with the maximum absorption of fluazinam. Thus the occurrence of inner filter effect led to the significant fluorescence quenching of MoS2 QDs. Additionally, fluorescent quenching efficiency of MoS2 QDs could be enhanced by the effects of π-π stacking, hydrogen bond and electrostatic interaction between fluazinam and MoS2 QDs, and these non-chemical bond responses also promoted the selectivity for fluazinam detection. Under the optimum conditions, the IFE-based fluorescent sensor exhibited a relative wide linear range from 50 nM to 25 μM with the LOD of 2.53 nM (S/N = 3). In addition, a paper-based sensor was established by cross-linking the MoS2 QDs into cellulose membrane for naked-eyed detection and digital analysis of fluazinam. The paper-based sensor presented a liner range from 10 μM to 800 μM for fluazinam detection with the LOD of 2.26 μM. Additionally, the acceptable recoveries were obtained for fluazinam detection in the spiked samples of tomato, potato and cucumber, indicating that the proposed method provided an effective sensing platform for real applications of fluazinam detection in food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Fluazinam
Molybdenum disulfide
Inner filter effect
Paper-based sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Paper-based sensing and nake-eyed and digital readout
Selectivity and stability of the MoS2 QDs sensor
Nake-eyed and digital readout using paper-based sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Graduate Scientific Research and Innovation Foundation of Chongqing
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and size distribution (B) of MoS2 quantum dots.
XPS spectrum (A) of MoS2 quantum dots. High resolution regions of S2d (B) and Mo3d (C). FT-IR spectrum of MoS2 quantum dots (D).
The fluorescence lifetime curves of MoS2 QDs solution and MoS2 QDs-fluazinam solution.
Fluorescence property of MoS2 quantum dots and absorption of fluazinam.
Fluorescence (A) of MoS2 quantum dots after responding with different concentrations of fluazinam and corresponding fitting curve (B).
(A) Fluorescence of MoS2 quantum dots after responding with various pesticides (fluazinam: 25 μM, others: 100 μM). (B) The net fluorescent responses caused by different pesticides.

Photographs of paper-based sensor before (A) and after (B) addition of fluazinam under ultraviolet light and corresponding standard digital image (C and D). RGB difference map of paper-based sensor (E) with various concentration of fluazinam (0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40, 0.60, 0.80, 1.00, 2.50 μM).
Linear fitting of between the EDΔRGB and the fluazinam concentrations ranging from 0.01 mM to 0.8 mM.
The sensing principle of IFE-based strategy for fluazinam detection.
The fluorescence lifetime of MoS2 QDs solutions in absence and presence of fluazinam.
A comparison of proposed sensor with other reported methods for fluazinam sensing.

row.entry: Triple Signal Outputs (Fluorescence, Nake-eyed recognition and IISV output)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of fluazinam in real applications.

head.ce:title: MoS2 QDs-Based sensor for measurement of fluazinam with triple signal output

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Those authors contribute equally to the article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, direct detection of fluazinam was realized using a fluorescent sensor using disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) via inner filter effect (IFE). The maximum excitation of as-prepared MoS2 QDs presented a complementary spectral-overlap with the maximum absorption of fluazinam. Thus the occurrence of inner filter effect led to the significant fluorescence quenching of MoS2 QDs. Additionally, fluorescent quenching efficiency of MoS2 QDs could be enhanced by the effects of π-π stacking, hydrogen bond and electrostatic interaction between fluazinam and MoS2 QDs, and these non-chemical bond responses also promoted the selectivity for fluazinam detection. Under the optimum conditions, the IFE-based fluorescent sensor exhibited a relative wide linear range from 50 nM to 25 μM with the LOD of 2.53 nM (S/N = 3). In addition, a paper-based sensor was established by cross-linking the MoS2 QDs into cellulose membrane for naked-eyed detection and digital analysis of fluazinam. The paper-based sensor presented a liner range from 10 μM to 800 μM for fluazinam detection with the LOD of 2.26 μM. Additionally, the acceptable recoveries were obtained for fluazinam detection in the spiked samples of tomato, potato and cucumber, indicating that the proposed method provided an effective sensing platform for real applications of fluazinam detection in food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluazinam, as a pyridinamine fungicide, presents an efficacious control of phytophthora blight with a relative low toxicity [1–4]. Although fluazinam is considered as a low-toxic pesticide, the excess use in agriculture still gives rise to an enormous threat to environment and food safety [5,6]. Studies have demonstrated that fluanizam could present a long-term retention in the soil and blade surfaces [7], which not only reduces the soil quality but also destroys the balance of the microbial population [8]. To make matters worse, the intakes of fluazinam in body can lead to asthma and dermatitis [9], and reduces the expression level of tyrosine hydroxylase [10,11]. Therefore, it is extremely imperative to establish effective and reliable methods to monitor fluazinam in agriculture and food safety.

Accordingly, fluazinam measurement can be realized by several analytical techniques including gas chromatography (GC) [6,12,13], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14–16], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [17,18], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [19]. Nevertheless, those strategies still involve in a number of ineluctable deficiencies, which include high expenditures, tedious sample-preparation, time-consuming, the need of large infrastructure and skillful operation, restricting their in-field applications.

As an effective sensing strategy, quantum dots based fluorescent sensors have drawn increasing concerns due to their outstanding advantages of simplicity, easy-modification, and high stability and sensitivity in the field analytical chemistry [20–24]. Particularly, few quantum dots based fluorescent sensors have been developed for fluazinam detection [25,26]. Especially, Wang et al. [25] developed a fluorescent sensor for fluazinam analysis utilizing fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CdTe QDs and porphyrin. Although this method presented good sensitivity, it involved in expensive porphyrin and the toxic CdTe QDs could cause secondary pollution. Zou et al. also presented a fluorescent strategy for direct measurement of fluazinam via the inner filter effect (IFE) [26]. The sensor exhibited excellent sensitivity and selectivity, but the requirement of high temperature (80 °C) and long detection time (22 min) limited its application. In our previous work, L-Cys–Cu NCs were applied for direct detection of fluazinam. However, L-Cys–Cu NCs also presented an obvious response for Hg2+, which also interfered fluazinam analysis [27].

The MoS2 QDs were first prepared by Splendiani et al., in 2010 [28]. Compared with other quantum dots, MoS2 QDs exhibit some excellent properties of low toxicity [29–31] and high stability [32,33]. Especially, the successful hydrothermal synthesis was realized by Wang et al. [33] and Gu et al. [34], which made MoS2 QDs be widely used to develop sensing platforms for detection glucose [35], ochratoxin A [36], dopamine [37] and ascorbic acid etc [38]. As far as we know, few relevant applications of MoS2 QDs in pesticide residues have been reported. In addition, IFE has been proved as a simple, sensitive and effective fluorescent detection method [24,39–41]. It likely happened when an absorber coexists with an analyte-independent fluorophore. The competitive absorption occurred when the excitation or emission of fluorescent probe overlapped with the absorption of target, resulting in the sufficient fluorescence quenching [42]. Therefore, just by matching the absorber-fluorophore pair in optical spectrum, the detection procedure based-on IFE was realized without any modification of fluorescent probe. In consideration of these, it becomes a reality to develop a fluorescent sensing system for fluazinam using MoS2 QDs based on IFE.

In the present work, MoS2 QDs served as a fluorescent sensor for detection of low-level fluazinam residues in vegetable samples. MoS2 QDs exhibited the emission of 404 nm after excitation at 322 nm. Meanwhile, fluazinam possessed the absorption peak at 347 nm. Thus the excitation of MoS2 QDs can be absorbed by fuazinam, resulting in the occurrence of IFE. The IFE led to the fluorescent extinction of MoS2 QDs. Such design realized the fluazinam detection. More importantly, MoS2 QDs were further cross-linked into cellulose membrane to develop a paper-based sensor. Fluazinam measurement was realized by direct droplet addition on the surface of the sensor. Photographs before and after response to fluazinam were taken under ultraviolet light and the digital data were analyzed by an Intelligent Identification System for Visual Chip (IISV) designed by our group. A color change map was automatically obtained from IISV system before and after the reaction with fluazinam, which generated ΔRGB data in red-green-blue and realized the visual detection of fluazinam. The digital readout for quantification of fluazinam was realized by calculating the Euclidean distance (ED) of ΔRGB to obtain the standard curve.

Sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), chitosan (90% deacetylated), l-cysteine, and quinine sulfate were purchased from Shanghai Tansoole Co., Ltd. (China). All pesticides used in the work were supplied by Chongqing Customs. Other conventional chemical reagents were ordered from Chuandong Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China). Deionized (DI) water with a resistivity of 18 KΩ was applied for preparation and dilution of solutions.

The optical properties of MoS2 QDs were confirmed by a fluorospectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, LS-55). The morphology of MoS2 QDs was characterized by a Tecnai G20 microscope (FEI). XPS was measured to identify the elements distribution on the surface of MoS2 QDs. XRD spectra were applied for crystallographic analysis of MoS2 QDs and implemented on an Empyrean XRD system (Bruker, Germany). The chemical groups of MoS2 QDs were investigated by FT-IR. The purified-MoS2 QDs solutions were concentrated by rotary evaporation at 70 °C in a negative pressure. The obtained solid MoS2 QDs were dried at 70 °C for 5 h and then ground with KBr powder under the irradiation of sodium lamp. The mixture powder was pressed to form a semitransparent slice and measured on a Nicolet iS50 infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). The fluorescence lifetime of MoS2 QDs was tested on a FLS-920 transient fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh). RGB values, color change files and standard difference maps were obtained from an Intelligent Identification System for Visual Chip (IISV) designed by our group. The operational principle and data processing process of IISV system were expatiated in detail in our previous studies [43–46].

A hydrothermal treatment procedure of sodium molybdate and l-cysteine was executed to synthesize MoS2 QDs according the previous study [33]. Briefly, 25 mL Na2MoO4 solutions (10 mg/mL) was adjusted to a certain pH of 6.5 using 0.1 M HCl. Then 50 mL l-cysteine (10 mg/mL) was added into above Na2MoO4 solutions. The intermixture were treated with a hydrothermal process at 200 °C for 36 h. The hydrothermal products were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 3min to wipe off large spots. The supernatant was purified with dialysis bag (1000 Da) and then stored at 4 °C under darkness for further use.

The following procedures were performed for fluazinam detection using IFE-Based fluorescent sensor. A well-distributed precursor was prepared by fully mixing 15 μL MoS2 QDs solutions, 2059.5 μL ethanol (99.9%) and 895.5 μL Tris-HCl solutions (10 mM, pH 11). After that, 30 μL fluazinam solutions with different concentrations responded with the precursor for 5 min. The fluorescence spectrum were recorded after excitation at 322 nm for further data analysis.

To evaluate the potential application for fluazinam analysis in real samples, tomato, potato and cucumber were tested using proposed method. Twenty grams vegetable samples were squeezed and added into three 250-mL beakers. Then 70 mL ethanol and 30 mL Tris-HCl solutions (10 mM) were successively added. After ultrasonication, the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 r/min for 15 min to detach bulky grains. Different concentrations of fluazinam (1, 10, and 20 μM) were added into these as-obtained solutions and then tested with as-prepared fluorescent sensor under optimized conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based sensing and nake-eyed and digital readout
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 5 mg chitosan and 10 mL as-prepared MoS2 QDs solutions were added into 20 mL ultra-pure water and then incubated at room temperature for 2 h under vigorous stirring. Cellulose membranes were immerged into stock solutions overnight. The solvents of MoS2 QDs-treated cellulose membranes were blotted with dry filter paper and then dried under 40 °C to obtained paper-based sensors. Fluazinam solutions (20 μL) with various concentrations were dropped on the test areas of MoS2 QD paper-based sensor. After dried naturally at room temperature, the photographs were taken by a smart phone under the ultraviolet excitation. The photographs were subsequently analyzed by the IISV system to obtain the standard digital images. By comparing the standard digital images in presence and absence of fuanizam, ΔRGB data of color change in red-green-blue values were obtained. A color difference map was generated from IISV system to realize the nake-eyed detection of fluazinam. The Euclidean distance (ED) of ΔRGB was calculated to quantify the concentration of fluazinam.

The morphology, chemical groups and elements, and optical properties of MoS2 QDs were investigated in detail. As seen from the TEM image in Fig. 1 , MoS2 QDs presented granules, and possessed a flake-like two-dimensional structure. As can be seen from the HRTEM, the parallel and ordered lattices were observed. It indicated as-prepared MoS2 QDs possessed a good crystallinity. The lattice space (0.23 nm) is consistent with the (0 0 6) plane of hexagonal MoS2. The size distribution image (Fig. 1B) revealed the size of MoS2 QDs of about 4–9 nm with an average diameter of 6 nm. Furthermore, the diffraction peaks interrelated with Mo, S, N, C and O were observed in XPS spectrum of MoS2 QDs (Fig. 2 A). Six single peaks were observed in high resolution spectrum of S2p (Fig. 2B). Two peaks at 162.26 and 162.24 eV were associated with S2p3/2, S2p1/2. And other four peaks at 163.56, 164.76, 168.0 and 169.14 eV indicated sulfur presented the likely existence of S2 2−, Sn 2− or S2O3 2− group. As can be seen from high resolution XPS of Mo3d region (Fig. 2C), three different peaks at 227.73, 228.73 and 232.44.8 eV were ascribed to S2s, Mo3d5/2, and Mo3d3/2. FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2D) was used to identify chemical groups of the MoS2 QDs. A wide and strong peak at 3369 cm−1 was related to O–H and N–H, demonstrating sufficient hydroxyls and amidogens were integrated in MoS2 QDs and presented a good water solubility. Two obvious peaks at 1561 cm−1 was indicative of in-plane stretching vibration of N–H. Peak at 1406 cm−1 was associated with symmetrical stretching vibration of CO. The absorption peak at 2550 cm−1 was not observed in the FT-IR spectra of MoS2 QDs compared with that of L-cys, indicating that a transformation of existing form –SH of L-cys to MoS2 QDs. Besides, the optical properties of MoS2 QDs were measured. It showed a blue emission (404 nm) after excitation at 322 nm (Figure S1). All these results obtained above presented outstanding performances of MoS2 QDs in view of dispersity, topography and structure.

The principle of proposed sensor is illuminated in Scheme 1 . The inner filter effect likely happened when an absorber coexists with an analyte-independent fluorophore. When the absorption of the absorber matches with the emission or excitation of fluorophore, the IEF can results in a sufficient fluorescence quenching [42]. As depicted in Fig. 3 , a considerable overlap in spectra was observed. Thus IFE occurred between MoS2 QDs and fluazinam led to a sufficient fading in fluorescence. To affirm the interactions between fluazinam and MoS2 QDs, fluorescence lifetime was measured in presence and absence of fluazinam. The mixture of fluazinam-MoS2 QDs system exhibited a consistent fluorescence decay curve with that of individual MoS2 QDs sample (Fig. 4 ). It indicated that fluazinam led to a static quenching of MoS2 QDs. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime was obtained through a double exponential function fitting of the test data using Origin 8.5 (function: y = y0 + A1*exp(-x/τ1) + A2*exp(-x/τ2), the fitting constants (A1 and A2) and the decay time (τ1 and τ2) were obtained by Origin 8.5). The average fluorescence lifetime (τav) was calculated by the formula: τav = (A1τ1+A2τ2.)/(A1+A2). As summarized in Table 1 , the fluazinam-MoS2 QDs system possessed an average fluorescence lifetime of 4.50 ns, which was close to that of individual MoS2 QDs sample (4.50 ns). The static quenching process was further confirmed by these results, illustrating the inner filter effect between fluazinam and MoS2 QDs. In addition, the abundant functional groups of –OH, –NH2, –SH and –COOH on the surface of MoS2 QDs presented three main kinds of responses between MoS2 QDs and fluazinam. (1) A strong electrostatic interaction occurred between aromatic rings of fluazinam and –OH, –NH2, –SH, –COOH. (2) Hydrogen bonds were produced between fluazinam and –OH, –NH2, –SH, –COOH. (3) Pyridyla and phenyl of fluazinam responded with –OH, –NH2, –SH, –COOH, and generated π-π stacking. These specific interactions promoted the sensitivity and selectivity of MoS2 QDs to detect fluazinam.

To promote the analytical performances, the influences of ratio of alcohol to water, pH and reaction time were investigated in detail. ΔF (ΔF = F–F0, the fluorescence change between the detection sample (F0) and the blank sample (F0)) was applied to evaluate the effects of different experimental conditions on fluazinam detection. Figure S2A depicted the effect of solvent ratio (alcohol to water (3:7–10:0)) on fluorescent response. The fluorescent response caused by fluazinam reached a maximum value with the ratio of alcohol to water increasing to 7:3. After that, a declined fluorescent response was observed with further increasing ratio of alcohol to water. It might be attribute that both hydrophilic groups and hydrophobic groups on the surface of the MoS2 QDs resulted in different fluorescent response with different ratios of alcohol to water. As shown in Figure S2B, the fluorescent responses changed with different pH of the solutions. The largest fluorescence quenching was obtained at the pH of 11. Thus it was beneficial to form the intermolecular forces between fluanizam and MoS2 QDs at a relatively high pH. As shown from the effect of reaction time in Figure S2C, it was an instantaneous response between the sensor and fluazinam. Therefore, 5 min was enough long for fluazinam detection.

We investigated the analytical performances of IFE-based sensor for different concentrations of fluazinam. As expected, MoS2 QDs showed a gradual fluorescence decrease with the increasing amount of fluazinam. As shown in Fig. 5 , concentration-dependent fluorescent response (F0–F1) was observed in the presence of fluazinam with the concentration from 50 nM to 25 μM. The linear relationship obeyed equation: F0–F1 = 23.0442 × CFA (μM) + 11.6555 (R2 = 0.9927). The low detection limit was calculated as 2.53 nM (S/N = 3). Compared with other reported methods summarized in Table 2 , proposed sensor presented the outstanding sensitivity and a much wider linear range, demonstrating the excellent performances on sensitive fluazinam analysis. In addition, the proposed methods could provide triple signal outputs for the detection of fluazinam and realized a more accurate recognition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the MoS2 QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity is also one of the key factors for a sensor. Therefore, a series of pesticides with analogous structure including omethoate (OM), glyphosate (Glp), parathion-methyl (PM), carbaryl (CB), isoprocarb (IP), malathion (MT), triadimefon (TA), carbendazim (CD), chlorothalonil (CR), chlorpyrifos (CP), cartap (CT) were selected to estimate the performance in selectivity of IFE-based sensor. A sufficient fluorescent decrease was caused by fluazinam of 25 μM (Fig. 6 ). Contrarily, other tested pesticides caused the negligible fluorescent response even at a high concentration of 100 μM. We evaluated the selectivity of proposed method for fluazinam detection in presence of other similar pesticides. The accordant results were obtained (Figure S3). We further measured the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum to investigate the selectivity of the IFE-based sensor. As can be seen from Fig. S4, only fluazinam showed a strong and wide absorption at 300–400 nm. Thus fluazinam could absorb the excitation of MoS2 QDs and resulted in the fluorescence decrease of MoS2 QDs, which guaranteed the specific response to fluazinam rather than other pesticides.

Additionally, persistent recording the fluorescence of MoS2 QDs was performed to assess the stability. As exhibited from Figure S5, MoS2 QDs were stored at 4 °C for eight weeks. The fluorescence value still remained the 91.8% of the original intensity. Besides, the reproducibility was investigated by testing different batches of IFE-based sensor on reaction with 25 μM fluazinam under optimal conditions. Ignorable changes was obtained in multiple tests (RSD 2.94%), indicating that the IFE-based sensor presented repeatable results for fluazinam analysis.

To verify the practical application, different samples of tomato, potato and cucumber spiked with fluazinam (1, 10 and 20 μM) were tested using the IFE-based sensor under the optimal conditions. As shown in Table 3 , the acceptable recoveries of spiked samples ranging from 97.6% to 102.9% were obtained with the maximum RSD of 4.18%. These results demonstrated the proposed IFE-based sensor presented a promising platform for fluazinam detection in real sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nake-eyed and digital readout using paper-based sensor

ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we have developed a fluorescent sensor in liquid phase and paper base to detect fluazinam in vegetable samples using MoS2 QDs. The fluazinam detection was realized by the inner filter effect between fluazinam and MoS2 QDs. MoS2 QDs with abundant functional groups of –OH, –NH2, –SH and –COOH presented the occurrence of π-π stacking, hydrogen bond and electrostatic interaction between fluazinam and MoS2 QDs, which promoted the sensitivity and selectivity. More importantly, MoS2 QDs were further applied for fabrication of paper-based sensor, which realized nake-eyed and digital readout for fluazinam detection. To demonstrate the practical application, tomato, potato and cucumber samples were successfully tested on the proposed sensor for fluazinam analysis. The experimental results indicated that the designed sensor is expected to be a new efficient approach for detection of fluazinam.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31171684), Graduate research and innovation foundation of Chongqing, China (Grant No. CYB18026), Solid-state Fermentation Resource Utilization Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (No. 2018GTJ010), Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (CSTC2015shmszxl20097, CSTC2018jcyjAX0062), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2019CDYGZD007), Brew Microorganisms Technology and Application of Key Laboratory Project in Sichuan Province (No. NJ2018-01), Chongqing Graduate Tutor Team Construction Project and the sharing fund of Chongqing University’s large equipment and for financial support. We also acknowledge the Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University for the characterizations of XRD, XPS, TEM and FT-IR.
